title: Minimum Distance between Two Substrings with Different Counts of Each Character

statement.markdown:
Given two substrings s1 and s2 in a string S, find the minimum distance between them. However, there is a catch - s1 and s2 must have different counts of each character. The counts are defined as the frequency of each character in the substring. If it is not possible to find such a pair, return 0.

Example:
S = abcdefg
s1 = ab
s2 = fg
The output should be 0 because s1 and s2 do not have a different count of each character (1 = 1).

Example:
S = aabcabbc
s1 = abc
s2 = bbb
The output should be 2 because s1 and s2 have different counts of each character (1 != 3).

The string S will contain only lowercase English letters, and the substrings s1 and s2 will be substrings of S.

You can assume that there are at least two substrings s1 and s2 with the same length.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be provided in 3 lines: S (the string), k1 (length of s1), k2 (length of s2)
statement.outputFormat: Output should be the minimum distance between s1 and s2.
statement.notes: Time and space complexity expectations: The solution should be able to handle the worst-case scenario of having 1 character repeated in the whole string (i.e., all characters are the same) in O(n) time and O(n) space. n is length of string S

examples.explanation: As mentioned in the example, s1 and s2 do not have a different count of each character (1 = 1), so the output is 0.
s1 and s2 have different counts of each character (1 != 3), so the output is 2.

hints.text: Create a hashmap to store the frequency of each character in the string S.
Use a sliding window to track the frequency of characters in s1 and s2.
Compare the frequency of each character in s1 and s2 to determine if they have different counts.

editorial.title: Solution to Minimum Distance between Two Substrings with Different Counts of Each Character
editorial.intuition:
The idea is to use a sliding window approach to track the frequency of characters in s1 and s2.
The key observation is that if s1 and s2 have different counts of each character, then it must be true that the frequency of each character in s1 does not match the frequency of the corresponding character in s2.
editorial.bruteForce: The time complexity of this approach is O(n * m * m), where n is the length of the string S and m is the maximum length of s1 and s2.
editorial.betterSolution: We can improve the time complexity to O(n + m) by observing that we can maintain a hashmap of the frequency of each character in the string S.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n + m) and a space complexity of O(n + m) due to the hashmap.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness:
The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the sliding window approach correctly calculates the frequency of characters in s1 and s2.
The key insight is that if s1 and s2 have different counts of each character, then it must be true that the frequency of each character in s1 does not match the frequency of the corresponding character in s2.
editorial.dryRun:
1. Read the input string S and the lengths of s1 and s2.
2. Create a hashmap to store the frequency of each character in the string S.
3. Use a sliding window to track the frequency of characters in s1 and s2.
4. Compare the frequency of each character in s1 and s2 to determine if they have different counts.
5. Output the minimum distance between s1 and s2.

{"variable":"s1Window","type":"Sliding Window","value":"Store frequency of each character in s1"}
{"variable":"s2Window","type":"Sliding Window","value":"Store frequency of each character in s2"}
